hero.title: TOMOCLOUD
hero.leadJa: 新しい自分が視える世界へ

vision.heading: Our Vision
vision.taglineJa: 視える化技術により全ての人が自分らしく生きることのできる社会を創造する
vision.taglineEn: Enabling authentic lives through the power of visualization.
vision.p1: For most of us, tracking our health day-to-day isn’t easy. Simple, continuous ways to monitor the body remain limited.
vision.p2: Tools to easily monitor the body and tissues are still limited.
vision.p4: At TOMOCLOUD, we visualize the invisible.

items.title: Act like an owner
items.text: 一人ひとりが自分事として課題に向き合い、責任を持って最後までやり抜く

items.title: Act on innovation
items.text: 最先端の技術を追求し、それを実用的なソリューションに変える

items.title: Act from zero
items.text: 前例のない挑戦を恐れず、小さな一歩から大きな変革を生み出す

items.title: Act as one
items.text: 多様な専門性を持つメンバーが協働し、個人では成し得ない価値を創出する

ogawa.title:
Project Assistant Professor, Takei Lab, Graduate School of Engineering, Chiba University
Also: Entrepreneur in Residence (EIR), Institute for Research Promotion (IMO), Chiba University
ogawa.subtitle: Ph.D. in Engineering, Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Chiba University
ogawa.positionNote:
Project Assistant Professor, Takei Lab, Graduate School of Engineering, Chiba University
Entrepreneur in Residence (EIR), IMO, Chiba University
ogawa.bio: Selected as a Tobitate! (Leap for Tomorrow) Japan representative; studied at the Centre for Regenerative Medicine, University of Edinburgh for one year (MSc period). Driving social implementation under the theme of “Medicine × Engineering,” developing a medical device using Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT) to address lymphedema after gynecologic cancer treatment. Aiming for “a world where you can see your new self,” advancing commercialization across medical, nursing, and healthcare fields. Selected for JST START (Project Promoting Startups) with Prof. Takei and colleagues.
achievements: 2019: Selected as Japan representative, World Youth Forum
2021: METI Japan Healthcare Business Contest – Excellence Award (and others)
2022: NEDO TCP Grand Prize; JSPS DC1; Chiba Univ. President/Dean/Chair Awards
2023: Early Ph.D. completion (5 papers), Best Presentation Award (Lymphedema Treatment Society), Research Studio finalist, JSPS PD
2025: AMED BRIDGE (Univ. of Tsukuba) S0; GTIE GAP Fund 2025 Entry Course; Beyond Japan Zero to X Program

takei.title: Professor, Graduate School of Engineering (also with Frontier Center for Biomedical Engineering), Chiba University
takei.subtitle: Ph.D. in Engineering, Waseda University
takei.positionNote: Professor, Graduate School of Engineering (Frontier Center for Biomedical Engineering), Chiba University
takei.bio: From 1995 to 2011, served at Nihon University (Dept. of Mechanical Engineering) as Research Associate, Lecturer, Associate Professor, and Professor, working on electrical measurement and multiphase flow dynamics. Led projects including JST programs and KAKENHI (C/B). Since 2011, Professor at Chiba University continuing research on bio/multiphase-flow electrical measurement. Expanded into plant and biomedical measurement via JST A-STEP projects; KAKENHI (A) advanced to thrombus visualization in blood flow. In 2018, selected for NEDO Advanced IoT project on AI-based early lymphedema detection using IoT EIT monitoring.
achievements: PI on multiple JST A-STEP and KAKENHI (A/B/C) projects
Invited talks at Lymphedema Society and JCS annual meetings
Multiple awards incl. NEDO Special Jury Prize & NEDO TCP 2017 Finalist

message.heading: Message
message.title: “Visualize the New Self”
paragraphs: While belonging to an engineering lab, I visited hospital wards from my student days to hear directly from patients and clinicians. One patient’s words—“Not seeing my body’s condition is what scares me most”—stayed with me, and I strongly wished to develop technologies that ease such unseen anxieties. The issues I witnessed felt not like someone else’s problems, but ones I should solve.

I also realized, deeply, that a patient is a person with everyday life. Everyone has had a life and will continue to have one—with roles as a patient, a family member, a friend, and a professional. I want to support all of those “selves.” That is why we shape technology not merely as a tool, but as a companion that stays close to daily life.

What we have honed is the power of visualization—Electrical Impedance Tomography (EIT). Each human tissue has unique electrical properties, which also vary with the body’s fluid composition (about 60% water). EIT is an innovative technology that visualizes these properties simply and with high fidelity.

This makes it possible to replace costly, burdensome examinations and to enable at-home monitoring that was previously difficult, with promising use cases for chronic diseases such as early detection and monitoring of lymphedema.

EIT is not just for visualization; it is a compass to see, understand, and take the next step. When the unseen becomes visible, people can move forward. By supporting that moment as a common language across medical, nursing, and healthcare domains, we will create a society where everyone can live authentically—and implement this Japan-originated model globally.
